# Introduction

Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome characterized by uncontrolled activation of macrophages and cytotoxic T cells, leading to a cascade of tissue destruction, multiorgan failure, and, if left untreated, death in 50--70% of cases [@Rose2019Recommendations]. Once considered an obscure curiosity of pediatric hematology, HLH has emerged as a clinical entity of growing relevance to adult medicine, driven by rising incidence, expanding etiological triggers, and a broadened understanding of its immunopathological spectrum. Population-based studies in England have demonstrated that the incidence of HLH quadrupled between 2003 and 2018, a trend attributable not only to heightened clinical awareness and improved diagnostic tools but also to the proliferation of immunomodulatory therapies and novel infectious triggers that predispose adults to immune dysregulation [@Emile2016Revised]. Despite this alarming trajectory, the diagnosis of HLH in adults remains fraught with difficulty: its clinical presentation---sustained fever, cytopenias, hyperferritinemia, hepatosplenomegaly, and coagulopathy---overlaps extensively with sepsis, multiorgan dysfunction syndrome, and other critical illnesses, frequently leading to delayed recognition and missed therapeutic windows [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic].

The historical roots of HLH stretch back nearly a century. In 1939, Scott and Robb-Smith first described a fatal syndrome of "histiocytic medullary reticulosis" characterized by systemic proliferation of histiocytes with prominent erythrophagocytosis in lymph nodes, spleen, and bone marrow. Thirteen years later, in 1952, Farquhar and Claireaux delineated a familial form of the disease occurring in siblings during infancy, establishing the hereditary basis that would later be elucidated at the molecular level through identification of specific cytolytic pathway gene defects. The revised classification of histiocytoses and neoplasms of the macrophage-dendritic cell lineages, published by @Emile2016Revised, formally positioned HLH alongside macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) within a unified nosological framework, recognizing both as manifestations of a shared pathophysiological continuum driven by defective immune regulation and excessive cytokine production.

The distinction between primary (genetic) and secondary (acquired) HLH remains a cornerstone of clinical classification, although the boundaries between these categories are increasingly recognized as permeable. Primary HLH results from biallelic loss-of-function mutations in genes essential for lymphocyte cytotoxic granule exocytosis, including *PRF1* (encoding perforin), *UNC13D* (encoding Munc13-4), *STX11* (encoding syntaxin 11), and *STXBP2* (encoding Munc18-2) [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab]. These mutations impair the ability of natural killer cells and cytotoxic T lymphocytes to eliminate target cells, resulting in persistent antigenic stimulation, unchecked T-cell activation, and a self-amplifying cytokine storm dominated by interferon-gamma, interleukin-18, interleukin-1-beta, and interleukin-6 [@Kaplanski2018Interleukin; @Dinarello2018Overview]. More recently, germline mutations in *HAVCR2*, encoding the immune checkpoint receptor TIM-3, have been identified as a cause of subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma with associated HLH, expanding the genetic landscape beyond classical familial disease [@Gayden2018Germline]. While primary HLH predominantly manifests in infancy and early childhood, with the landmark HLH-2004 cooperative study enrolling 369 children and reporting a 5-year survival probability of 61% [@Bergsten2017Confirmed], emerging whole-exome sequencing data reveal that heterozygous variants in cytolytic pathway genes may lower the threshold for HLH in adults who encounter sufficient immune provocation [@Maffucci2016Genetic; @Crayne2019The].

Secondary HLH in adults arises in the context of potent immune triggers, most commonly infections (particularly Epstein-Barr virus and other herpesviruses), hematologic malignancies (especially T-cell and NK-cell lymphomas), and autoimmune or autoinflammatory disorders, in which case the syndrome is designated macrophage activation syndrome (MAS-HLH) [@Rose2019Recommendations]. The epidemiology of adult HLH is notable for a bimodal age distribution, with incidence peaks in younger adults aged 16--30 years---often associated with primary immunodeficiency or viral triggers---and again in older adults aged 56--70 years, in whom malignancy-associated HLH predominates and comorbid conditions may further compromise immune homeostasis. @Shakoory2016Interleukin demonstrated that among 763 adults with sepsis and multiorgan dysfunction, a subgroup of 43 patients (5.6%) exhibited features of macrophage activation syndrome, and that treatment with the interleukin-1 receptor antagonist anakinra was associated with significantly improved 28-day survival in this population (65.4% vs. 35.3%; hazard ratio 0.28; *p* = 0.0071), underscoring both the prevalence of HLH-spectrum disease in critically ill adults and the therapeutic relevance of its identification. The diagnostic challenges are compounded by the fact that the HLH-2004 criteria, which require fulfillment of five of eight clinical and laboratory parameters, were developed and validated exclusively in pediatric cohorts and are not formally validated for adults [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Knaak2020Hemophagocytic]. In a cohort of 2,623 adult critically ill patients with hyperferritinemia, @Knaak2020Hemophagocytic found that the HScore, with a cutoff of 168, yielded a sensitivity of 100% and specificity of 94.1% for HLH diagnosis, providing modestly superior diagnostic accuracy compared with the HLH-2004 criteria in this population.

The decade spanning 2016 to 2026 has fundamentally transformed the landscape of adult HLH. The COVID-19 pandemic brought hyperinflammatory syndromes to the forefront of global medical consciousness, as a subset of patients with severe SARS-CoV-2 infection developed cytokine storm physiology with features overlapping secondary HLH and MAS [@Mehta2020COVID; @Merad2020Pathological; @McGonagle2020The]. @Soy2020Cytokine highlighted that COVID-19-associated macrophage activation syndrome, driven by impaired viral clearance, dysregulated interferon responses, and excessive pyroptosis, can culminate in a clinical picture closely resembling HLH, necessitating collaboration between intensivists, hematologists, rheumatologists, and immunologists. @Webb2020Clinical developed and validated a six-criterion scale for COVID-19-associated hyperinflammatory syndrome (cHIS) in 299 hospitalized patients, demonstrating that 54% met two or more criteria and that higher cHIS scores were independently associated with mortality (odds ratio 1.6; *p* = 0.002) and need for mechanical ventilation (odds ratio 4.3; *p* < 0.0001). Simultaneously, the rapid expansion of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has introduced a new iatrogenic trigger for HLH: severe cytokine release syndrome can evolve into fulminant HLH, a recognized though uncommon complication reported across multiple CAR-T products [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @Brudno2019Recent]. In a systematic review and meta-analysis of 7,604 patients receiving CAR-T therapy, @Cordas2024A found that HLH, together with cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity, accounted for 11.5% of nonrelapse deaths cumulatively, with overall nonrelapse mortality reaching 6.1--10.6% depending on the disease entity treated. Immune checkpoint inhibitors, now a cornerstone of oncologic therapy, have similarly expanded the pool of adults at risk for secondary HLH through their capacity to unleash immune-related adverse events that can mimic or trigger macrophage activation [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated; @ShimabukuroVornhagen2018Cytokine]. These developments, combined with the approval of emapalumab (anti-interferon-gamma antibody) for primary HLH---in which 63% of previously treated patients achieved a response [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab]---and growing evidence supporting JAK inhibitors [@Jamilloux2019JAK] and IL-6 blockade with tocilizumab [@Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic; @Kotch2019Tocilizumab] in hyperinflammatory states, have collectively reshaped the therapeutic armamentarium available to clinicians managing adult HLH.

Despite these advances, the management of HLH in adults continues to rely heavily on extrapolation from pediatric protocols, most notably the etoposide-based HLH-94 and HLH-2004 regimens, which may result in unnecessary toxicity when applied without age-appropriate modifications [@Ehl2018Recommendations; @Rose2019Recommendations]. A comprehensive, evidence-based synthesis that integrates recent developments is therefore urgently needed to guide clinicians confronting this diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. The objectives of this systematic review are as follows:

1. To summarize the current understanding of HLH pathogenesis in adults, with particular attention to cytokine-mediated mechanisms and genetic susceptibility.
2. To evaluate existing diagnostic criteria and scoring systems, including the HLH-2004 criteria and HScore, for their performance in adult populations.
3. To catalog the expanding spectrum of etiological triggers, including infections, malignancies, autoimmune diseases, CAR-T cell therapy, and immune checkpoint inhibitors.
4. To critically appraise treatment strategies for adult HLH, encompassing conventional immunosuppressive regimens, targeted biologic therapies, and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
5. To assess the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the recognition, diagnosis, and management of HLH-spectrum disorders.
6. To identify knowledge gaps and propose directions for future research aimed at improving outcomes in this high-mortality condition.
